title: Crosslinking of loose insulating powders
abstract: Described herein are materials and methods useful in the field of insulation, including building materials, refrigeration, cryogenics, and shipping, amongst others. Advantageously, the provided materials and method provide low thermal conductivities and increased mechanical strength, allowing for efficient insulating in a diverse range of applications. The provided materials and methods include individual particles connected by a polymer network that links individual particles and may include hollow or evacuated capsules and various strengthening agents.
claims:
Claims CLAIMS What is claimed is: 1. An insulating material comprising: a plurality of insulating particles; a polymer crosslinking the plurality of insulating particles, wherein the polymer comprises an adhesive; wherein the plurality of insulating particles have a packing density greater than or equal to 50%. 2. The insulating material of claim 1, wherein the adhesive comprises a cyanoacrylate. 3. The insulating material of claim 1 or 2, wherein the insulating material has a thermal conductivity less than or equal to 9 mW/m-K. 4. The insulating material of any of claims 1-3, wherein the polymer further comprises a boron-containing strengthening agent. 5. The insulating material of claim 4, wherein the boron-containing strengthening agent comprises triisopropyl borate, boron trichloride, trimethyl borate or a combination thereof. 6. The insulating material of any of claims 1-6 further comprising a plurality of reinforcing fibers. 7. The insulating material of claim 6, wherein the reinforcing fibers comprises cellulose, a plant material, a second polymer, glass or a combination thereof. 8. The insulating material of claim 6 or 7, wherein the reinforcing fibers is chemically bonded to the polymer, physically bound to the polymer, dispersed in void space between the insulating particles, or a combination thereof. 9. The insulating material of any of claims 1-8, wherein the insulating material has a maximum compressive stress greater than or equal to 0.10 MPa. 10. The insulating material of any of claims 1-9, wherein the insulating material has a maximum compressive strength greater than or equal to 0.48 MPa at 10% deformation. 11. The insulating material of any of claims 1-10, wherein the insulating material has a shear strength greater than or equal to 0.05 MPa. 12. The insulating material of any of claims 1-11, wherein the insulating material has a tensile strength greater than or equal to 0.05 MPa. 13. The insulating material of any of claims 2-12, wherein the cyanoacrylate comprises methyl cyanoacrylate, ethyl cyanoacrylate, butyl cyanoacrylate, octyl cyanoacrylate or a combination thereof. 14. The insulating material of any of claims 2-13, wherein the cyanoacrylate comprises ethyl 2-cyanoacrylate and octyl cyanoacrylate. 15. The insulating material of any of claims 1-14, wherein the polymer encapsulates an outer surface of the plurality of insulating particles. 16. The insulating material of any of claims 1-15, wherein the polymer does not fully encapsulate an outer surface of the plurality of insulating particles. 17. The insulating material of any of claims 1-16, wherein the polymer is deposited on an outer surface of the plurality of insulating particles at a thickness selected from the range of 5 to 10 monolayers. 18. The insulating material of any of claims 1-17, wherein the plurality of insulating particles comprise a fluoropolymer coating. 19. The insulating material of any of claims 1-18, wherein the plurality of insulating particles comprise fumed silica. 20. The insulating material of any of claims 1-19, wherein the plurality of insulating particles comprise a plurality of hollow capsules. 21. The insulating material of claim 20, wherein the plurality of hollow capsules have a diameter selected from the range of 5 nm to 1 cm. 22. The insulating material of claim 21 wherein the plurality of hollow capsules comprise: a plurality of small hollow capsules; and a plurality of large hollow capsules. 23 The insulating material of claim 22, wherein the plurality of small hollow capsules have a diameter selected from the range of 0.01 mm to 0.03 mm. 24. The insulating material of any of claim 22 or 23, wherein the plurality of large hollow capsules have a diameter selected from the range of 0.1 mm to 1 mm. 25. The insulating material of any of claims 22-24, wherein the plurality of small hollow capsules have an average diameter with a ratio selected from the range of 1 :2 to 1:10 with respect to an average diameter of the large hollow capsules. 26. The insulating material of any of claims 20-25, wherein the hollow capsules have an internal pressure of about 1 atm. 27. The insulating material of any of claims 20-25, wherein the hollow capsules have an internal pressure less than or equal to 0.001 atm. 28. The insulating material of any of claims 20-27, wherein the hollow microspheres comprise hollow glass microspheres. 29. A method comprising: providing a plurality of insulating particles with a packing density greater than or equal to 60%; depositing or having water on a least a portion of an outer surface of the plurality of insulating particles; and reacting at least one type of cyanoacrylate monomer assisted by the water to generate a polymer network, thereby crosslinking the plurality of insulating particles and generating an insulating material; wherein the insulating material has a thermal conductivity less than or equal to 0.1 W/m-K. 30. The method of claim 29, wherein the cyanoacrylate is provided in a vapor phase. 31. The method of claim 29 or 30 further comprising reacting a boron-containing strengthening agent with the cyanoacrylate, the polymer network or a combination thereof. 32. The method of claim 31, wherein the boron-containing strengthening agent is provided in a vapor phase. 33. The method of any of claims 29-32 further comprising providing a reinforcing fiber, wherein the reinforcing fiber is chemically bonded to the polymer network, physically 

 bound to the polymer network, contained in a void space between the insulating particles or a combination thereof. 34. The method of any of claims 29-33 further comprising coating the insulating particles with a fluoropolymer, thereby altering the surface hydrophobicity of the plurality of insulating particles. 35. The method of any of claims 29-34, wherein the plurality of insulating particles comprise fumed silica. 36. The method of any of claims 29-35, wherein the plurality of insulating particles comprise hollow capsules. 37. The method of claim 36, wherein the plurality of insulating particles comprise hollow glass microspheres. 38. An insulating material comprising close-packed hollow capsules, wherein each capsule is at least partially covered with a layer of a polymer adhesive having a substantially uniform thickness of at least 1 monolayer which bonds the hollow capsules together. 39. The insulating material of claim 38, wherein the layer of polymer adhesive covers at least 1% to 100% of a surface area of each capsule. 40. The insulating material of claim 38 or 39, wherein a layer of polymer adhesive is interposed between neighbouring capsules. 41. The insulating material of claim 38 or 39, wherein at least some of the hollow capsules are in direct contact with one another. 42. The insulating material of any of claims 38-41, wherein the polymer adhesive comprises at least one polymer adhesive selected from poly cyanoacrylates, polyvinyl alcohols, polypyrroles, poly(alkylenedioxythiophenes), polyimides, polyazomethines, polyureas, polyamides, polyimide-polyamides, polythioureas, polyethylene terephthalate, and alucones. 43. The insulating material of any of claims 38-41, wherein the polymer adhesive comprises a cyanoacrylate adhesive. 44. The insulating material of any of claims 38-43, wherein the thickness of the layer of polymer adhesive is selected from the range of 1 to 10 monolayers. 45. The insulating material of any of claims 36-44, wherein the polymer adhesive and hollow capsules are covalently bonded with a strengthening agent, e.g., a boron- containing moiety. 46. The insulating material of any of claims 36-45, wherein the hollow capsules are at least partially coated with a low-emissivity coating. 47. The insulating material of claim 46, wherein the low-emissivity coating comprises a conducting polymer, preferably polypyrrole or poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene). 48. The insulating material of claim 46 or claim 47, wherein the low-emissivity coating comprises a conducting oxide semiconductor, preferably tin oxide, indium oxide, titanium oxide, zinc oxide, boron tin trioxide or a combination thereof. 49. The insulating material of any of claims 36-48, wherein the hollow capsules comprise first and second pluralities of hollow capsules of different size distributions; the hollow capsules of the first plurality having a mean diameter which is greater than the mean diameter of the hollow capsules of the second plurality; the two pluralities of hollow capsules being substantially heterogeneously mixed to increase packing density. 50. The insulating material of claim 49, wherein the packing density of hollow capsules is selected from the range of 50% to 99%. 51. The insulating material of claim 49 or 50, wherein the ratio of mean diameters of the larger hollow capsules to the smaller hollow capsules is between 2: 1 and 10:1. 52. The insulating material of any of claims 49 -51, wherein the ratio of the number of smaller capsules to larger capsules is between 5:1 and 15:1. 53. The insulating material of any of claims 36-52, wherein the hollow capsules are evacuated. 54. The insulating material of any of claims 36-53, wherein the insulating material further comprises silica filler particles which are disposed in interstices between the hollow glass spheres. 55. The insulating material of claim 54, wherein each of the silica particles is at least partially covered with a layer of polymer adhesive which bonds the fumed silica particles to the hollow glass spheres. 56. An insulating composite comprising the insulating material according to any preceding claim and a substrate. 57. A building material selected from drywall, plywood board, insulating panels for use in refrigerators/freezers, shipping containers and construction, comprising the insulating material according to any of claims 36 to 56. 58. A method of making an insulating material comprising: a) close packing a plurality of hollow capsules into a mold, b) infusing the mold with at least one gas phase precursor that polymerizes to form a polymer adhesive, and c) causing or allowing the precursor to polymerize on exterior surfaces of the hollow capsules, thereby bonding the hollow capsules together. 59. The method of claim 58, wherein the hollow capsules have two distinct size distributions, and the method comprises substantially homogeneously mixing the hollow capsules prior to packing in the mold. 60. The method of claim 58 or 59, further comprising the step of substantially homogeneously mixing the hollow capsules with fumed silica particles prior to packing in the mold. 61. The method of any of claims 58-60, further comprising infusing the hollow capsules in the mold with a strengthening agent which is adapted to form covalent bonds with the hollow capsules and polymer adhesive. 62. The method of any of claims 58-61, wherein the at least one precursor comprises at least one homobifunctional precursor. 63. The method of claim 62, wherein the at least one homobifunctional precursor is selected from one or more of diamines, dienes, diisocynates, diisothiocyanates, dialdehydes, dianhydrides, diols, e.g. glycols, dithiols, dihalides, e.g. dichlorides di- or tri- alkoxysilanes, and alkylenedioxythiophenes. 64. The method of claim 62 or 63, wherein two or more homobifunctional precursors are infused into the mold. 65. The method of any of claims 62 to 64, further comprising reacting the at least one precursor in the presence of an accelerator, initiator, catalyst and/or one or more additional precursors. 66. The method of claim 65, wherein the accelerator, initiator, oxidant, catalyst and/or additional precursor(s) is co-delivered simultaneously or sequentially with the at least one precursor. 67. The method of claim 65, wherein the accelerator comprises water vapor which is present on the surface of the hollow capsules. 68. The method of claim 65, wherein the hollow capsules have modified exterior surfaces to cause or allow polymerization of the at least one precursor. 69. The method of any of claim 58-68, wherein the at least one precursor is selected from one or more of a Ci-ioalkyl cyanoacrylate monomer, vinyl acetate, pyrrole and 3,4- ethylenedioxythiophene. 70. The method of claim 69, wherein the Ci-ioalkyl cyanoacrylate monomer is selected from one or more of methyl cyanoacrylate, ethyl cyanoacrylate, butyl cyanoacrylate and octyl cyanoacrylate. 71. The method of any of claims 58-70, further comprising causing or allowing the at least one precursor to flow through the hollow capsules in the mold prior to bonding. 72. The method of any of claims 58-71, wherein the at least one precursor is infused under a pressure below atmospheric pressure. 73. The method of any of claims 58-72, wherein the at least one precursor is infused at a temperature above the boiling point of the polymer adhesive. 74 The method of claim 73, wherein the at least one precursor is infused at a temperature below about 200 Â°C. 75. The method of any of claims 58-74, further comprising at least partially coating the hollow capsules with a low-emissivity coating. 76. The method of claim 75, wherein the low-emissivity coating comprises a conducting oxide semiconductor, preferably tin oxide, indium oxide, titanium oxide, zinc oxide, boron tin trioxide or a combination thereof, which is applied to the hollow capsules by atomic layer deposition.
